symbols: slumped lifeless body — the weight of death upon the incarnate God, the price of redemption
powerful musculature rendered lifeless — divine strength laid low by willing sacrifice
arm study at right margin — the artist meditating on Christ's human form, the mystery of incarnation
description: A pencil or chalk sketch on light paper showing a muscular male figure slumped forward and to the right, seen from behind and slightly above. The torso is powerfully rendered with careful attention to the musculature of the back, shoulders, and arms. The figure's head hangs forward heavily. In the upper right margin, a smaller study of a bent arm is visible. The drawing is unfinished, with the lower portions lightly indicated.
theological_description: Michelangelo's preparatory study for the Pietà captures the paradox at the heart of Christian theology — the almighty Creator rendered in death's heaviness. The powerful musculature, so carefully observed, emphasizes the full humanity of the incarnate Word: 'Since the children have flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death' (Hebrews 2:14). Christ's slumped form embodies the kenosis — the self-emptying of God who, 'being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage, but made himself nothing' (Philippians 2:6-7). The weight of the dead body speaks to Isaiah's prophecy: 'Surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows' (Isaiah 53:4). Even in preparatory form, this study reveals the theological truth that Christ's death was fully real, fully physical — not a phantom or illusion, but the genuine laying down of a genuine human life.

scripture_refs.reason: Christ shared in flesh and blood to destroy death — the full physicality of his death is rendered here
The kenosis — God emptying himself and becoming obedient to death, visible in the lifeless body
He bore our griefs — the weight of humanity's sin made visible in the heaviness of the dead form
